figures.caption: Figure 1: Our Proposed Agent Framework. The agent learns internal representations via encoders, the QMoPSSM, and a decoder. Imagined future states and preferences guide both policy learning and value estimation.

Table 1: We report the evaluation success rate of the last 100 episodes of MYOE (ours) as compared to other model/RL baselines. Cyan cells represent the best performing agents within the corresponding task. Note that “Drm” is short for “DreamerV3.”

Figure 2: QMoP-SSM Learning Architecture. QMoP separates latent learning into two interconnected parallel processes: “representation” and “preference” learning. While the model predicts future latent given previous actions, future preference state trajectories are guided by provided and learned goals.

Table 2: We report the evaluation success rate of the last 100 episodes of MYOE (ours) in comparison to other baselines. Cyan cells represent the best performance agents within the corresponding task.
Figure 4: Our proposed MYOE solving the “reach” task when integrated into the “7bot” robot (top) and our agent model solving the “block picking” task when integrated into the PX100 robot (bottom).
Figure 3: Cumulative reward (y-axis) across 1 million interaction/training steps (x-axis) for different agents.
